evals.prompt: A support ticket has this JWT attached and I need to know what's actually in it before I dig further: eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0NTY3ODkwIiwibmFtZSI6IkpvaG4gRG9lIiwiaWF0IjoxNTE2MjM5MDIyfQ.SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c — can you tell me the claims and whether it's expired?
evals.expected_output: Uses `jwtop decode` (not verify, since no secret/key was given) to show header/claims, correctly notes there's no `exp` claim so it can't expire, and doesn't claim the signature was validated.
assertions: Uses `jwtop decode` on the token rather than `jwtop verify` (no secret/key was supplied, so signature can't be checked)
Correctly lists all three claims: sub=1234567890, name=John Doe, iat=1516239022
Correctly notes the token has no `exp` claim, so expiration can't be determined from the token alone
Does not claim the signature was verified or the token is 'valid'

evals.prompt: We're doing an internal security review of our billing-service API. Here's one of the JWTs it issues: eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJleHAiOjE3ODM3ODQzMjIsImlhdCI6MTc4Mzc3NzEyMiwiaXNzIjoiYmlsbGluZy1zZXJ2aWNlIiwicGxhbiI6ImZyZWUiLCJyb2xlIjoidXNlciIsInN1YiI6ImFwaS11c2VyLTQ4MiJ9.54Cc1d2AeMzOVr3H-u0sjA2PbcCm6AUmbUebTQwKcXQ — can you check if the signing secret is guessable, and give me a markdown report I can drop into our findings doc?
evals.expected_output: Runs `jwtop crack` offline (no --url, since no server was mentioned and this is an internal review of the token itself) with `--format markdown`, finds the weak secret ("secret"), and surfaces the recovered secret plus a markdown-formatted report.
assertions: Uses `jwtop crack` (the scanner) rather than manually reimplementing dictionary attack logic
Runs offline (no --url) since no server/endpoint was mentioned
Uses --format markdown (or otherwise produces a markdown-formatted report as explicitly requested)
Correctly identifies and states the recovered weak secret is "secret"

Final artifact/response is valid enough markdown to drop into a findings doc, and the recovered secret value is surfaced to the user even though jwtop's markdown/html/terminal report formats omit it (only --format json includes it)

evals.prompt: We run our own Node.js auth service (auth.internal.example) that issues ES256 tokens, and I want to check it isn't vulnerable to CVE-2022-21449 before we ship. Here's a sample token from our staging environment: eyJhbGciOiJFUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJleHAiOjE3ODM3ODA3MjIsImlhdCI6MTc4Mzc3NzEyMiwiaXNzIjoiYXV0aC5pbnRlcm5hbC5leGFtcGxlIiwicm9sZSI6InVzZXIiLCJzdWIiOiJub2RlLWFwcC11c2VyLTE5In0.g2ow6EOMLCS2mqz20vyN9LsUjvh_wxjhEkNGl06f2MwE2ZjPU6XTVxB6V65NgbXbu2Bq2K3tq26IQC1PMH0ajg — can you generate a PoC token I can replay against our staging verifier to confirm it rejects it?
evals.expected_output: Recognizes ES256 as the relevant precondition for the psychic-signature bug, runs `jwtop exploit psychicsig` on the given token to produce the all-zero r=0/s=0 PoC token, and explains what a staging verifier accepting it vs. rejecting it would mean. Since this is the user's own staging service, no additional authorization confirmation should be needed.
assertions: Identifies (via decode or otherwise) that the token's alg is ES256
Uses `jwtop exploit psychicsig` on the given token rather than hand-crafting a forged JWT
Explains what it means if staging accepts vs. rejects this PoC
Does not block on or demand extra authorization confirmation — the user already stated this is their own staging service
